perturbation.original_text: 5.1 This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and take effect as an enforceable contract in accordance with the laws of the State of New York governing such agreements, without regard to conflicts-of-law principles thereof that would require applicability of any other law.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and take effect as an enforceable contract in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without regard to conflicts-of-law principles thereof that would require applicability of any other law.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from New York to California can introduce inconsistencies if the contract contains clauses that are valid under New York law but not under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code 229 states that actions for the collection of due and unpaid wages can be maintained without regard to the existence of any private agreement to arbitrate. The original contract includes a clause waiving a jury trial. If this contract was enforced in California, the jury trial waiver may be deemed unenforceable with respect to wage claims due to conflict with CA Labor Code 229.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, LAB 229. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Labor Code - LAB DIVISION 2. EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 1. COMPENSATION [200 - 452] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 1. Payment of Wages [200 - 273] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. General Occupations [200 - 244] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 229. Actions to enforce the provisions of this article for the collection of due and unpaid wages claimed by an individual may be maintained without regard to the existence of any private agreement to arbitrate. This section shall not apply to claims involving any dispute concerning the interpretation or application of any collective bargaining agreement containing such an arbitration agreement. (Added by Stats. 1959, Ch. 1939.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law of the agreement from New York to California. The Law Explanation and Scraped Law Snippet 1 state that California Labor Code 229 allows actions for unpaid wages to be maintained regardless of any private agreement to arbitrate. The Law Explanation also points out that the original contract contains a jury trial waiver, which might be unenforceable in California for wage claims due to conflict with CA Labor Code 229. Thus, changing the governing law introduces a contradiction because a clause valid under New York law (jury trial waiver) may be deemed unenforceable under California law, specifically regarding wage claims, creating a legal contradiction as defined by the prompt.

perturbation.original_text: 5.2 Any notices or other correspondence (hereinafter collectively referred to as “correspondence”) required or permitted to be given hereunder shall be in writing and shall be sent by postage prepaid first class mail, courier or facsimile or delivered by hand to the party to whom such correspondence is required or permitted to be given hereunder, and shall be deemed sufficient upon receipt when delivered personally or by courier, overnight delivery service or confirmed facsimile, or three (3) Business Days after being deposited in the regular mail as certified or registered mail (airmail if sent internationally) with postage prepaid, if such notice is addressed to the party to be notified at such party’s address or facsimile number as set forth below:
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2 Any notices or other correspondence (hereinafter collectively referred to as “correspondence”) required or permitted to be given hereunder shall be in writing and shall be sent by email only to the party to whom such correspondence is required or permitted to be given hereunder, and shall be deemed sufficient upon sending by email. There is no recourse if the email is not received.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting notice to email only and disclaiming responsibility for receipt may conflict with legal requirements for certain types of notices, particularly those involving legal claims or significant contractual obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Electronic Transactions Act (UETA), as adopted in New York
perturbation.law_explanation: UETA, as adopted in New York, requires agreement between parties to conduct transactions by electronic means. Making email the *only* acceptable form of communication, and disclaiming recourse for non-receipt, arguably contradicts the principles of UETA which requires demonstrable consent to electronic communication for certain legal and contractual purposes.  The disclaimer of recourse contradicts good faith dealing.

perturbation.justification: The modified text restricts communication to email only and disclaims responsibility for receipt, which is a contradiction. The Law Explanation states that UETA requires agreement between parties to conduct transactions by electronic means, and the disclaimer of recourse contradicts good faith dealing. The scraped law snippet 1 shows that the requested entry for NYS Open Legislation could not be found. Given this context, and the fact that making email the *only* acceptable form of communication, and disclaiming recourse for non-receipt, arguably contradicts the principles of UETA which requires demonstrable consent to electronic communication for certain legal and contractual purposes. This makes the contradiction fall under the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition: 'Inconsistencies arise when time-sensitive obligations in a contract do not align with legal requirements. A legal contradiction in this category happens when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.'

perturbation.original_text: 7.  Termination. 7.1 The provisions of this Agreement shall continue in full force and effect at all times until the earliest to occur of (i) the filing of an exchange offer, offering memorandum or disclosure statement with respect to the 2016 Notes or Convertible Notes, provided that , to the extent CEDC and the Recipient continue discussions on the terms of a Restructuring following such filing, this period shall extend until the filing of an amended exchange offer, offering memorandum or disclosure statement reflecting such discussions or (ii) 9:00 a.m. (GMT) on March 5, 2013, whereupon this Agreement shall terminate and be of no further force or effect (the “ Termination Date ”), other than with respect to the rights and obligations of CEDC and Recipient per Section 7.2 hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.  Termination. 7.1 The provisions of this Agreement shall continue in full force and effect at all times until the earliest to occur of (i) the filing of an exchange offer, offering memorandum or disclosure statement with respect to the 2016 Notes or Convertible Notes, provided that , to the extent CEDC and the Recipient continue discussions on the terms of a Restructuring following such filing, this period shall extend until the filing of an amended exchange offer, offering memorandum or disclosure statement reflecting such discussions or (ii) 9:00 a.m. (GMT) on March 5, 2013, whereupon this Agreement shall terminate and be of no further force or effect (the “ Termination Date ”), other than with respect to the rights and obligations of CEDC and Recipient per Section 7.2 hereof. This agreement may be unilaterally terminated by CEDC at any time, without notice.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a clause allowing unilateral termination by one party without notice can be problematic, especially if it affects obligations related to confidential information and securities laws. Such a provision might be challenged if it's deemed to violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY UCC § 1-304 states that 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' A clause allowing unilateral termination *without notice* could be seen as a violation of this duty of good faith, particularly if the other party (Recipient) has reasonably relied on the continuation of the agreement.

Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.justification: The added clause in the Changed Text allows CEDC to unilaterally terminate the agreement at any time without notice. This directly contradicts NY UCC § 1-304, which imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of every contract. Unilateral termination without notice can be interpreted as a breach of this duty of good faith, particularly if the Recipient has reasonably relied on the agreement's continuation, as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states, 'Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.'

perturbation.original_text: 4. Remedies . The Recipient acknowledges that a breach of any of the terms of this Agreement may cause irreparable harm to CEDC for which CEDC may not be adequately compensated by money damages. Accordingly, the Recipient agrees that, in addition to all other remedies available to CEDC, including any member of the CEDC Group, in an action at law, in the event of any breach or threatened breach by the Recipient of the terms of this Agreement, CEDC shall, without the necessity of proving actual damages or posting any bond or other security, be entitled to seek temporary and permanent injunctive relief, including, but not limited to, specific performance of the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Remedies . The Recipient acknowledges that a breach of any of the terms of this Agreement may cause irreparable harm to CEDC for which CEDC may not be adequately compensated by money damages. Accordingly, the Recipient agrees that, in addition to all other remedies available to CEDC, including any member of the CEDC Group, in an action at law, in the event of any breach or threatened breach by the Recipient of the terms of this Agreement, CEDC shall, without the necessity of proving actual damages, be entitled to seek temporary and permanent injunctive relief, and the Recipient hereby consents to immediate garnishment of wages without any prior notice or hearing.
perturbation.explanation: Consenting to immediate wage garnishment without notice or a hearing violates due process rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Wage Garnishment Law, 15 U.S. Code § 1673
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S.C. § 1673 protects employees from excessive wage garnishment. However, more significantly, the clause 'Recipient hereby consents to immediate garnishment of wages without any prior notice or hearing' violates due process rights which require notice and opportunity to be heard before property (wages) are seized. While the federal law addresses limits on the amount garnished, it does not address the due process violation created by waiving notice and hearing rights.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a clause where 'Recipient hereby consents to immediate garnishment of wages without any prior notice or hearing,' which directly violates due process rights. The perturbation explanation and the law explanation both highlight that this addition contradicts due process, which requires notice and an opportunity to be heard before seizing property (wages). While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is 'None,' the absence of supporting snippets does not negate the strong contradiction identified. The original contract allows for remedies, including injunctive relief, upon breach, but the modified version escalates to immediate wage garnishment without due process, creating a legal contradiction as it removes fundamental legal protections.
